story_title: The GLD Launch — How a Single ETF Put Gold in 50 Million Portfolios
summary: When the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (ticker: GLD) began trading on the New York Stock Exchange on 18 November 2004, it became the fastest exchange-traded fund in history to reach $1 billion in assets; within three years it had accumulated more gold than the central banks of most nations and fundamentally reshaped who owned gold.
recommended_arc: Hook: for the first time in history, retail investors can buy gold by clicking a button — no vault, no bullion dealer. Rising: GLD hits $1 billion in three days; hedge funds pile in. Climax: within two years GLD holds more gold than the sovereign reserves of China at the time. Payoff: gold demand permanently democratised; the WGC's master plan to grow gold as an asset class succeeds beyond all projections.

verified_facts.claim: The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSE: GLD) began trading on the New York Stock Exchange on 18 November 2004; it was sponsored by the World Gold Council and managed by State Street Global Advisors.
GLD attracted $1.5 billion in assets within its first three trading days, setting a record for the fastest-ever new ETF launch to cross $1 billion — a record that stood for years.
By November 2007, GLD held approximately 600 tonnes of gold — more than the combined sovereign gold reserves of China at that time — making it one of the world's largest holders of physical gold.

GLD reached a peak of approximately 1,353 tonnes of gold under management in December 2012, representing a total asset value of over $77 billion; this made it, at times, the second-largest ETF in the world by assets under management, behind only the SPDR S&P 500.

Before GLD's launch, the primary means for ordinary investors to own gold were physical bullion, coins, or gold-mining stocks; GLD's structure — each share represented one-tenth of a troy ounce of gold backed by physical bullion held in HSBC's London vault — made gold accessible through any brokerage account.

The World Gold Council, which conceived and co-sponsored GLD, had spent approximately $10 million developing the ETF product concept over several years, working with legal and regulatory advisors to navigate SEC and NYSE requirements before the 2004 launch.
